groups.name: 4-Cylinder MIVEC Petrol
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): The MIVEC petrol family is known for timing chain stretch and tensioner failure, particularly on higher-mileage 4B1 engines, plus oil consumption issues on 2.0L/2.4L variants. Our rebuilt units include new timing chains, tensioners, guides, and upgraded piston rings where applicable.

groups.name: 4-Cylinder Turbo Petrol
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): The 4B40 turbo petrol engine is relatively modern but can experience wastegate actuator issues and carbon buildup on intake valves due to direct injection. Our rebuilt units include valve cleaning and wastegate inspection.

groups.name: 4D5 & 4N1 DI-D Diesel (1993–2024) – Highest Volume Commercial & 4x4
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): DI-D diesel engines suffer from EGR valve clogging, DPF blockage (Euro 5/6), injector failure, and turbocharger wear — especially on high-mileage L200 and Shogun 4x4s. Our rebuilt units include ultrasonic injector cleaning, new injector seals, EGR delete-ready intake cleaning, and upgraded turbo cartridges where specified.

groups.name: 6G7 Series V6 Petrol
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): The 6G7 V6 family is known for timing belt failures (interference engine — belt replacement critical every 60k miles), lifter tick, and oil leaks from valve cover gaskets. Our rebuilt units include new timing belt kits (water pump, tensioner, idlers) and resealed top-end gaskets.

groups.name: Legacy 4G Series Petrol
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): The 4G63/4G64 family is robust but suffers from crankshaft walk (early Evo models), timing belt failure (interference engine), and oil starvation in high-performance applications. Our rebuilt performance engines include upgraded bearings, ARP fasteners, and revised oiling systems.

commonProblems.h2: Common Mitsubishi Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Mitsubishi’s UK lineup—dominated by the L200 pickup, Outlander, Shogun, and ASX—has a reputation for mechanical toughness, but high-mileage diesel engines (4D56, 4N15, 4M41) and petrol direct-injection units (4B11, 4J12) develop predictable, costly failures. Diesel owners face EGR clogging, turbo wear, and DPF issues, while petrol and PHEV variants bring timing chain stretch and oil consumption. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point—plus a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common failures, what they cost to fix at a dealer vs. a specialist, and when replacement is the only smart move.

problems.h4: 4N15 & 4D56 Turbo & DPF Failure – Why High-Mileage Diesel Pickups Fail
problems.affectedModels: Mitsubishi L200 (2005–2019, 4D56), L200 Series 5 (2015–present, 4N15), Outlander DI-D (2007–2013, 4D56)
problems.rootCause: The EGR system recirculates soot-heavy exhaust gas, clogging intake manifolds and sticking turbo vanes. Simultaneously, short journeys prevent DPF regeneration, leading to ash blockages, back-pressure damage, and turbo failure.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – EGR & DPF Clean / Turbo Actuator |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Forced DPF regen, EGR valve removal & ultrasonic clean, actuator replacement where possible | 10,000–20,000 miles if underlying cause (short journeys) not corrected; only suitable pre-failure |
| New or reconditioned turbo, oil feed line replacement, new gaskets & oil change | 40,000–60,000 miles; viable for a vehicle worth £4,000+ |

| Supply & fit of rebuilt engine with uprated EGR cooler, cleaned inlet manifold, new turbo oil feed, 12-month warranty | 60,000+ miles; often cheaper than dealer turbo+DPF repairs on high-mileage examples |

problems.recommendation: On an L200 worth less than £4,000 with both turbo and DPF faults, replacement is almost always cheaper than main dealer repairs. If the turbo has failed and sent debris into the sump, do not fit a new turbo alone – you need a full engine replacement or a professional bottom-end flush. For a low-mileage Outlander, the specialist EGR/turbo actuator fix is the best value.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 4N15 and 4D56 engine prices for your L200 or Outlander

problems.h4: 4B11 & 4J12 Timing Chain Stretch – The Silent Petrol & PHEV Weakness
problems.rootCause: The timing chain tensioner loses oil pressure, allowing chain slack. Combined with variable valve timing (MIVEC) sprocket wear, the chain jumps teeth, causing poor running, rattling on start-up, and eventual valve-to-piston contact.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace oil-fed tensioner, inspect chain slack, new gasket, oil & filter change | Only viable before any rattling occurs (preventative); rarely worth doing alone |
| Complete timing chain kit (chains, guides, tensioner), cam and crank sprockets, front crank seal | 60,000+ miles; appropriate for any Outlander or ASX with rattle on cold start |
| Supply & fit of rebuilt engine with uprated chain kit, new MIVEC actuators, bench tested, 12-month warranty | Best for engines that have jumped timing and may have bent valves |

problems.recommendation: If your Outlander or ASX rattles for longer than two seconds on cold start, do not delay – a full specialist chain kit replacement is £700-£1,100 well spent. Once the engine runs poorly or fails to start, dealer diagnostics alone can exceed £200. For a vehicle worth £3,000–£5,000, a rebuilt exchange engine is often the safer long-term bet.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 4B11 or 4J12 engines with uprated timing kits

problems.h4: 4M41 & 4M40 Crankshaft & Bearing Failure – The Shogun / Pajero Catastrophe
problems.affectedModels: Mitsubishi Shogun / Pajero (1999–2015, 4M41 3.2 DI-D), Shogun Pinin (2000–2006, 4M40 2.8 TD)
problems.rootCause: Oil starvation due to sludge build-up in the pickup pipe or failed balance shaft bearings. The crankshaft thrust washers disintegrate, allowing axial crank movement that wrecks the block. This is rarely recoverable.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | New crankshaft (if salvageable), bearings, oil pump, balance shaft delete or replacement | 30,000–50,000 miles; only if crank journal is undamaged. High risk on older 4M41. |

| Supply & fit of fully rebuilt engine with upgraded oil pump, balance shaft delete option, new timing components, 12-month warranty | 60,000+ miles; the only reliable solution for a Shogun you plan to keep |

problems.recommendation: On a Shogun with the classic “crankshaft pulley wobble” or deep knocking from the bottom end, do not throw money at a partial rebuild. Specialist crank-on-block repairs almost never last. A full rebuilt exchange engine with balance shaft elimination is the industry-standard fix. For a Shogun worth under £3,000, we would honestly advise breaking the vehicle for parts.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 4M41 and 4M40 exchange engine prices with balance shaft delete

problems.h4: 4B12 Oil Consumption & PCV Failure – The Overlooked 2.4 Petrol Issue
problems.rootCause: The positive crankcase ventilation (PCV) valve sticks closed, building crankcase pressure that forces oil past piston rings and valve stem seals. Owners top up oil repeatedly, but the real damage is carboned rings and scuffed bores.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace PCV valve, intake manifold carbon clean, oil & filter change with engine flush | If caught before ring damage, this stops oil consumption in ~70% of cases |

repairOptions.tier: | Major – Piston Rings & Valve Seals (engine out) |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove engine, replace piston rings, re-cut valve seats, new seals, head gasket set | 50,000+ miles; viable on a low-mileage Outlander worth £4,000+ |
| Supply & fit of rebuilt engine with new rings, PCV system, and timed correctly, 12-month warranty | 60,000+ miles; often cheaper than a dealer major repair once labour is included |

problems.recommendation: If your 4B12 is using a litre of oil every 600 miles but still runs smoothly, start with a specialist PCV valve and de-carbon service – it’s the cheapest diagnostic step. If compression is low or bores are scored, a rebuilt exchange engine is more cost-effective than a dealer’s “major repair” quote, which rarely includes the required cylinder honing.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 4B12 engines with new piston rings and PCV system

problems.group: 4N14 Diesel Injector & High-Pressure Pump Failure (2.2 DI-D)
problems.h4: 4N14 Diesel Injector & High-Pressure Pump Failure (2.2 DI-D)
problems.rootCause: The Delphi fuel injection system’s high-pressure pump sheds microscopic metal particles, contaminating injectors and the fuel rail. The first sign is poor cold starting, followed by misfires and diesel in the engine oil.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Injector Return Flow Test & Clean |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove injectors, ultrasonic clean, replace copper washers, return flow test | Only diagnostic – rarely a permanent fix on contaminated systems |

repairOptions.tier: | Major – Replace HP Pump & One Injector |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | New high-pressure pump, one new injector (worst offender), fuel system flush, new filter | 30,000–40,000 miles; high risk of repeat failure if all injectors are not addressed |
| Supply & fit of rebuilt engine with new HP pump, full injector set, flushed fuel rail, 12-month warranty | Essential if diesel has heavily diluted engine oil and wiped bearings |

problems.recommendation: On any 4N14 with a confirmed HP pump failure, replacing the pump and a single injector is gambling. Contaminated fuel systems require a complete injector set and a professional rail flush. Once diesel contamination has thinned the engine oil, the crankshaft bearings are at immediate risk – we strongly recommend a rebuilt exchange engine instead of chasing component failures.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 4N14 engine prices with new HP pumps and full injector sets

problems.group: 3B20 & 4A92 MIVEC Oil Control Ring Seizure (1.3 & 1.5 Petrol)
problems.h4: 3B20 & 4A92 MIVEC Oil Control Ring Seizure (1.3 & 1.5 Petrol)
problems.rootCause: The low-tension oil control rings on the pistons become clogged with carbonised oil, losing their ability to scrape the cylinder walls. The result is high oil consumption (1 litre per 600 miles) and blue smoke on overrun.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove engine, replace all piston rings, hone cylinders, new gaskets, oil pump seal | 50,000+ miles; viable on a well-maintained Colt or Space Star |
| Supply & fit of rebuilt engine with new oil control rings, MIVEC timing chain, complete gasket set, 12-month warranty | 60,000+ miles; competitive pricing makes this attractive vs. major repair |

problems.recommendation: On a Colt or Space Star worth £1,500–£2,500, a specialist piston ring replacement (£1,100–£1,700) is borderline. For only a few hundred more, a rebuilt exchange engine with a full warranty is the smarter, lower-risk choice. Do not buy a used salvage engine – you are buying the same weak oil rings again.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 3B20 and 4A92 engines with upgraded oil control rings

engineTypes.h2: Mitsubishi Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Mitsubishi engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Mitsubishi Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price – ideal for high‑mileage L200 and Shogun diesels with EGR/DPF wear.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,800 – £3,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Mitsubishi Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as reinforced timing chains (4B1 MIVEC), uprated turbo oil feed lines (4N15 DI‑D), and AMC cylinder heads (4M40) – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Mitsubishi Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available. Popular for budget‑conscious Colt, Space Star and ASX repairs.

types.title: Mitsubishi Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for Lancer Evolution performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine for a long‑term keeper.

types.title: Mitsubishi Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn’t had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models (e.g., early Carisma, Galant, Colt) where a full recon isn’t economical.

types.title: Mitsubishi Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Mitsubishi specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty – essential for L200 and Shogun owners without a local workshop.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Mitsubishi engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Mitsubishi model today.

engineSizes.h2: Mitsubishi Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Mitsubishi engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.8L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.8-litre diesel is rare in Mitsubishi’s UK lineup, appearing primarily in the late-1990s Carisma and early Colt variants. These indirect-injection units (4D68) were economical but underpowered by modern standards, and most have now been replaced or scrapped. Owners searching for a 1.8 diesel replacement typically find that upgrading to a younger 2.0-litre or 2.2-litre engine from a later model is more cost-effective than sourcing a direct 4D68 exchange. Engine code(s): 4D68 (1.8 TD) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Carisma (1995–1999), Colt (1996–2000) Production years: 1995–2000 Common failure points: Cylinder head cracking; injector pump seal failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 1.8L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Mitsubishi’s 2.0-litre diesel engines powered the Carisma and early L200 pickups through the 1990s, using the robust 4D68T unit. These mechanically injected engines are simple to repair but suffer from injection pump wear and head gasket failure with age. Most UK specialists now recommend rebuilding the existing unit rather than sourcing a used example, given that most salvage engines have already exceeded 150,000 miles. Engine code(s): 4D68T Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Carisma (1995–2000), L200 (1990–1996) Production years: 1990–2000 Common failure points: Injection pump seal leaks; head gasket failure; turbo wear (L200)
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2-litre DI-D diesel (4N14) arrived in the 2013 Outlander and later the L200 Series 6, replacing older 2.0-litre and 2.3-litre units. This advanced common-rail engine improved economy and torque but introduced a well-documented weak point: the high-pressure fuel pump (Delphi system) can disintegrate, sending metal debris through injectors and the fuel rail. Once contamination occurs, a full engine replacement or complete fuel system overhaul is required. Engine code(s): 4N14 Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Outlander (2013–2019), L200 (2016–present), Eclipse Cross (2018–2020) Production years: 2013–present Common failure points: Delphi HP pump failure (metal contamination); injector seizure; diesel-in-oil dilution
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.3L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.3-litre diesel, fitted to the Shogun Sport and some Delica imports, sits between the 2.2-litre and 2.5-litre offerings. This 4M40 variant is essentially a stroked version of the 2.5-litre, sharing many components but with different crankshaft and pistons. UK availability is limited – most owners needing a 2.3 diesel replacement instead fit a complete 2.5-litre 4M40 engine or rebuild their original unit with new piston rings and bearing shells. Engine code(s): 4M40 (2.3 TD variant – rare in UK) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun Sport (1998–2005), Delica (import) Production years: 1998–2005 Common failure points: Crankshaft thrust washer failure; oil pump seal leaks
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 2.3L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.5-litre 4D56 turbo diesel is one of Mitsubishi’s most iconic and long-running engines, powering the L200, Shogun, and Delica from the 1980s through to the mid-2010s. Its simplicity and mechanical robustness made it a favourite among farmers and off-roaders, but high-mileage units suffer from timing chain rattle, injector pump leaks, and cylinder head cracks. Surprisingly, a fully rebuilt 4D56 is often cheaper than a used example, given the engine’s age and the availability of aftermarket parts. Engine code(s): 4D56 (2.5 DI-D / TD) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi L200 (1996–2015), Shogun / Pajero (1991–2006), Delica (import) Production years: 1986–2015 Common failure points: Cylinder head cracking (pre-2000); injector pump seal failure; timing chain stretch (later DI-D)
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.8L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.8-litre 4M40 turbo diesel is synonymous with the Shogun / Pajero (1994–2006) and Shogun Pinin, offering strong low-end torque for towing and off-road use. These engines are mechanically durable but have a critical weakness: the crankshaft thrust washers can disintegrate, causing axial crank movement that destroys the block. Once knocking or crank pulley wobble appears, a full rebuilt exchange engine with balance shaft elimination is the only reliable solution. Engine code(s): 4M40 (2.8 TD) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun / Pajero (1994–2006), Shogun Pinin (2000–2006), Delica L400 (import) Production years: 1994–2006 Common failure points: Crankshaft thrust washer failure; oil starvation from sludge; turbocharger seal failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 2.8L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre diesel is exceptionally rare in UK Mitsubishis, appearing only in certain imported Delica L400 and Japanese-market Pajero variants. This 4M40-based unit is essentially a long-stroke 2.8-litre with a different crankshaft and pistons, offering marginal torque gains at the expense of parts availability. For UK owners, rebuilding the existing 3.0-litre or converting to a 2.8-litre engine is more practical than sourcing a direct replacement. Engine code(s): 4M40 (3.0 TD – rare JDM variant) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Delica (import), Pajero (JDM import) Production years: 1995–2000 (import only) Common failure points: Parts scarcity; crankshaft bearing failure (same as 2.8L)
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 3.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.2-litre DI-D (4M41) replaced the 2.8-litre in the Shogun from 2000 onwards, offering common-rail injection and significantly improved refinement. This engine is widely respected for its longevity, but high-mileage examples (120,000+ miles) suffer from injector seal leakage, timing chain rattle, and – in rare cases – crankshaft bearing failure if oil changes have been neglected. Rebuilt 4M41 engines with upgraded oil pumps and timing components are the standard specialist remedy. Engine code(s): 4M41 (3.2 DI-D) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun / Pajero (2000–2015), Shogun Sport (2005–2010) Production years: 2000–2015 Common failure points: Injector seal leakage (carbon blow-by); timing chain tensioner wear; oil pump seal failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi 3.2L diesel engines

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Mitsubishi’s 1.0-litre 3A90 three-cylinder petrol engine powered the entry-level Space Star (2012–2020) and European Colt (2004–2008). These lightweight MIVEC units are surprisingly durable, but high-mileage examples suffer from valve timing solenoid failure and oil consumption due to clogged piston rings. A replacement 3A90 is relatively affordable, and many UK specialists stock reconditioned units with new timing chains and oil rings. Engine code(s): 3A90 (1.0 MIVEC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Space Star (2012–2020), Colt (2004–2008) Production years: 2004–2020 Common failure points: Oil ring seizure (oil consumption); MIVEC solenoid failure; timing chain stretch
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.1L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.1-litre petrol engine (3A91) is a stroked version of the 1.0-litre, fitted to the Colt and Space Star primarily in European markets. UK examples are less common, but owners who have them appreciate their fuel economy and simple timing chain design. The same oil ring and MIVEC issues affect the 1.1-litre unit, and replacement engines – either used or reconditioned – are widely available through UK Mitsubishi specialist breakers. Engine code(s): 3A91 (1.1 MIVEC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Colt (2004–2012), Space Star (2012–2015) Production years: 2004–2015 Common failure points: Oil control ring seizure; MIVEC solenoid failure; camshaft position sensor failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.1L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.3-litre petrol family spans two distinct generations: the older 4G13 (from the 1990s Colt and Carisma) and the later 3B20 MIVEC (2004–2013 Colt). The 3B20 is the more common replacement candidate today, suffering from the same oil control ring seizure as its larger 1.5-litre sibling (4A92). A rebuilt 3B20 with new rings, timing chain, and MIVEC sprocket is a cost-effective long-term solution for Colt owners. Engine code(s): 4G13 (1.3), 3B20 (1.3 MIVEC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Colt (1995–2013), Carisma (1995–1999), Space Star (2012–2020 – 3B20) Production years: 1995–2020 Common failure points: 3B20 – oil ring seizure, timing chain stretch; 4G13 – head gasket failure, carburettor issues
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre 4A92 MIVEC engine is one of Mitsubishi’s most common modern petrol units, fitted to the Colt, ASX, Space Star, and Lancer. Its smooth power delivery and reliability are let down by a single persistent fault: low-tension oil control rings that clog with carbon, causing heavy oil consumption (up to 1 litre per 600 miles). A rebuilt 4A92 with new piston rings, a cleaned cylinder head, and a fresh timing chain will typically outlast the original engine. Engine code(s): 4A92 (1.5 MIVEC), 4G15 (older 1.5 – Colt 1990s) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Colt (2004–2013), ASX (2010–2017), Space Star (2012–2016), Lancer (2008–2015) Production years: 2004–2020 Common failure points: Oil ring seizure (high oil consumption); MIVEC actuator rattle; ignition coil failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre 4G18 petrol engine appeared in the Carisma, late Colt, and Lancer across the late 1990s and 2000s. Simpler than later MIVEC units, the 4G18 is reliable but prone to camshaft position sensor failure and minor oil leaks from the rocker cover and sump. The Eclipse Cross also received a 1.6-litre petrol variant (4B40) – a turbocharged direct-injection engine with its own set of carbon deposit and injector issues. Engine code(s): 4G18 (1.6), 4B40 (1.6 DI Turbo – Eclipse Cross) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Carisma (1995–2000), Colt (2000–2004), Lancer (2000–2007), Eclipse Cross (2018–2021) Production years: 1995–2021 Common failure points: 4G18 – camshaft sensor failure; 4B40 – intake valve carbon deposits (direct injection), injector failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8-litre petrol family includes the 4G93 (found in the Carisma and Lancer) and the later 4B40 (Eclipse Cross) and 4J10 (ASX). The 4G93 is a simple, robust engine that suffers from distributor wear and minor oil leaks. The more modern 4B40 (1.5L) and 4J10 are less common replacement candidates in the UK due to lower sales volumes. Most 1.8-litre replacement queries come from Carisma or Lancer owners whose engines have suffered head gasket failure beyond economical repair. Engine code(s): 4G93 (1.8), 4J10 (1.8 MIVEC), 4B40 (1.8? – note: 4B40 is 1.6 turbo; check application) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Carisma (1995–2000), Lancer (1995–2007), ASX (2010–2015 – 4J10) Production years: 1995–2015 Common failure points: 4G93 – head gasket failure, distributor wear; 4J10 – MIVEC solenoid failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre 4B11 engine is Mitsubishi’s most important modern petrol unit, powering the Lancer (including the Ralliart and Evolution X), Outlander, ASX, and even the early Eclipse Cross. Known for its robust bottom end, the 4B11 is let down by timing chain stretch (especially on higher-mileage units) and MIVEC actuator rattle. For Evo X owners, a rebuilt 4B11 with upgraded head studs and timing components is the standard remedy after track use or head gasket failure. Engine code(s): 4B11 (2.0 MIVEC), 4G63 (older 2.0 – Evo IV–IX), 4J11 (2.0 MIVEC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Lancer (2008–2017), Outlander (2007–2013), ASX (2010–2017), Eclipse Cross (2018–2020), Evo X (2008–2015 – 4B11T turbo) Production years: 1994–2020 (4G63), 2007–2020 (4B11/4J11) Common failure points: 4B11 – timing chain stretch, MIVEC rattle; 4G63 – crankshaft thrust bearing failure; 4J11 – oil consumption
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.2-litre petrol engine is exceptionally rare in UK Mitsubishis, appearing only in certain Japanese-market Delica and Pajero imports (usually the 4G64-based 2.2-litre). For UK owners, specialist rebuilds are often the only practical route for 2.2-litre petrol replacement, given that used examples are almost impossible to source locally. Engine code(s): 4G64 (2.2 – rare JDM variant) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Delica (import), Pajero (JDM import) Production years: 1990–1998 (import only) Common failure points: Parts scarcity; timing belt failure (interference engine)
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 2.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.4-litre 4B12 petrol engine is the larger sibling to the 4B11, fitted to the Outlander, ASX, Lancer (including Ralliart models), and the US-market Galant. Like the 4B11, timing chain stretch is a known issue, but the 4B12 also suffers from pronounced PCV valve failure, leading to crankcase pressure build-up and heavy oil consumption. A specialist PCV replacement and intake decarbonisation can cure oil use if caught early; otherwise, a rebuilt 4B12 with new rings and timing components is required. Engine code(s): 4B12 (2.4 MIVEC), 4G64 (older 2.4) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Outlander (2007–2013), ASX (2010–2017), Lancer (2008–2015 – Ralliart) Production years: 2007–2017 (4B12) Common failure points: Timing chain stretch; PCV valve failure (oil consumption); MIVEC actuator rattle
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 2.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre V6 petrol engine (6G72) powered the Shogun, Shogun Pinin, and late 1990s Galant in the UK. This single-cam, 12-valve V6 is smooth and durable but suffers from oil leaks from the valve cover gaskets and camshaft end plugs. Timing belt replacement is critical – it is an interference engine, and belt failure bends all valves. Rebuilt 6G72 engines are available from specialist Mitsubishi V6 rebuilders, complete with new timing belts and water pumps. Engine code(s): 6G72 (3.0 V6 SOHC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun / Pajero (1991–2000), Shogun Pinin (2000–2006), Galant (1996–2002) Production years: 1991–2006 Common failure points: Timing belt failure (catastrophic); valve cover oil leaks; camshaft end plug leaks
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 3.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.5-litre V6 (6G74) appeared in the Shogun (2000–2006) and the Pajero Evolution (limited numbers). A development of the 6G72 with increased bore and stroke, the 6G74 shares many of the same weaknesses: oil leaks, timing belt vulnerability, and occasional valve guide wear. For Shogun owners, a rebuilt 6G74 with upgraded timing components and multi-layer steel (MLS) head gaskets is the recommended replacement route. Engine code(s): 6G74 (3.5 V6 DOHC / SOHC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun / Pajero (2000–2006), Pajero Evolution (limited) Production years: 2000–2006 Common failure points: Timing belt failure; valve guide wear (oil smoke on startup); head gasket failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 3.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 3.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.8-litre V6 (6G75) is the largest petrol engine fitted to a UK-market Mitsubishi, found in the Shogun (2006–2015) and the Lancer Evolution X (MR model, Japan-only). This 24-valve SOHC V6 is torquey and refined, but timing chain stretch (not belt) is a known weakness at higher mileages, along with oil consumption from stuck rings. Replacement 6G75 engines are expensive due to low UK availability – most owners instead rebuild their existing unit with a new timing chain, guides, and piston rings. Engine code(s): 6G75 (3.8 V6 SOHC) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Shogun (2006–2015) Production years: 2006–2015 Common failure points: Timing chain stretch; oil ring seizure; camshaft position sensor failure
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi 3.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi 2.0L Hybrid Engines (PHEV)
items.description: The Outlander PHEV (2014–present) uses a 2.0-litre 4B11 petrol engine paired with two electric motors (one front, one rear). The petrol engine acts primarily as a generator, only driving the wheels under heavy load or when the battery is depleted. Unlike conventional engines, PHEV engines often suffer less wear, but high-mileage examples face the same 4B11 timing chain stretch if the engine has run frequently without electric assistance. Replacement PHEV powertrains are specialist fit – the hybrid system requires professional high-voltage handling. System / motor code(s): 4B11 (2.0 MIVEC petrol) + front motor (Y4F1) + rear motor (Y4M1) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV (2014–present) Production years: 2014–present Important notes: High-voltage safety; battery health critical; hybrid system resync required after engine replacement
items.cta: Enquire about Mitsubishi 2.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Mitsubishi Electric Motors (BEV)
items.description: Mitsubishi’s only fully electric vehicle sold in the UK is the i-MiEV (2010–2016), a city car with a 16kWh battery and a 25kW permanent magnet synchronous motor. Electric motor failure is extremely rare, but bearing whine and motor controller (inverter) faults have been reported. Replacement motors are available from Japanese breakers, but most UK specialists recommend a bench-tested used unit over a full rebuild due to the high cost of re-winding the stator. System / motor code(s): Y4F1 (i-MiEV drive motor) Compatible models (UK): Mitsubishi i-MiEV (2010–2016) Production years: 2010–2016 Important notes: Inverter failure more common than motor failure; battery degradation is the primary concern

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Mitsubishi Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Mitsubishi engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Mitsubishi specialists.

entries.title: 4B11 – 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) The 4B11 powered the Lancer (2008–2017), ASX (2010–2012), Outlander (2007–2013), and Delica (2007–present). This 147–240bhp engine suffers from timing chain stretch and tensioner failure on high‑mileage units (80,000–120,000 miles), plus oil consumption on higher‑mileage variants. Our rebuilt units include a new timing chain kit with upgraded tensioner guides and revised piston rings to address oil control.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4B11 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4B12 – 2.4L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) Fitted to the Outlander (2007–2013), Lancer (2008–2017), and Galant. The 2.4L 4B12 shares the same timing chain vulnerability as the 4B11, with oil consumption also reported on engines exceeding 100,000 miles. Quality rebuilt units include a full timing overhaul, new piston rings, and a resealed oil pump assembly.

entries.title: 4J11 – 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) Found in the ASX (2010–2024) and Outlander (2013–2020). The 4J11 is a simplified evolution of the 4B1 family, but still experiences timing chain stretch on extended oil change intervals (factory 20,000‑mile schedule). Our rebuilt units include a new chain kit, tensioner, and upgraded oil control valve.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4J11 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4J12 – 2.4L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) Powering the Outlander (2013–2020) and later ASX models. Similar timing chain wear patterns to the 4J11, plus occasional VVT sprocket failure from oil sludge. Rebuilt units include new VVT units, chain kit, and a full oil system flush.

entries.title: 4N14 – 2.2L DI‑D Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Outlander (2012–2020) and Eclipse Cross (2018–2020). Common failures include DPF blockage (Euro 5/6 models), EGR valve carbon seizure, and injector seal failure causing diesel contamination in oil. Our rebuilt engines include a new DPF, cleaned EGR system, and reconditioned injectors with new seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4N14 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4N15 – 2.4L DI‑D Diesel
entries.description: The 4N15 powers the L200 (2015–2024). High‑mileage examples suffer from turbocharger wear (oil feed pipe sludge), EGR clogging, and DPF soot overload when used for short journeys or low‑speed off‑roading. Our rebuilt units include an upgraded turbo oil feed line, new injectors, and a cleaned intake manifold.

families.name: 4D5 & 4M4 Series (Legacy Diesel – Still High Replacement Demand)

entries.title: 4D56 – 2.5L DI‑D Diesel
entries.description: The legendary 4D56 equipped the L200 (pre‑2015), Delica, and Shogun/Pajero (pre‑2000). Catastrophic failures include cylinder head cracking (between valve seats) and turbo seal failure from EGR‑induced oil sludge. Our rebuilt units feature an upgraded AMC cylinder head (stronger than OEM) and a new turbocharger with cleaned oil system.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4D56 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4M40 – 2.8L Turbo Diesel
entries.description: Powering the Shogun/Pajero (1990s–2005) and Delica. Known for cylinder head cracking due to overheating (thin sections between valves) and injection pump wear from debris. Rebuilt units include a new AMC cylinder head, reconditioned injection pump, and a full cooling system inspection.

entries.title: 4M41 – 3.2L DI‑D Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Shogun/Pajero (2000–2015) and late L200 variants. This robust engine’s most common failures are injector seal leakage (diesel into oil) and turbocharger wear from EGR sooting. Bottom ends are tough, but high‑mileage units need injector and turbo attention. Our rebuilt units include new injector seals (uprated), a reconditioned turbo, and a cleaned EGR system.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4M41 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4A92 – 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) Powering the ASX (2010–2024), Lancer, and Colt. A generally reliable engine, but can suffer from timing chain stretch on extended oil change intervals. Our rebuilt units include a new timing chain kit and upgraded tensioner.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4A92 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 3A92 – 1.2L Petrol
entries.description: (MIVEC) Found in the Mirage and Space Star (2012+). Simple, robust engine with no major inherent failures—replacement usually follows neglect or accident damage. Rebuilt units include a full gasket set and new timing chain.

entries.title: 6G72 – 3.0L V6 Petrol
entries.description: Equipped the 3000GT/GTO, Shogun, and Delica. Known for timing belt failure (interference engine—catastrophic if belt snaps) and lifter tick on early hydraulic lash adjusters. Our rebuilt units include a new timing belt kit (water pump, tensioner, idlers) and rebuilt lifters.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 6G72 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 6G74 – 3.5L V6 Petrol
entries.description: Found in the Shogun, Delica (1993–2000), and Magna/Verada. Similar timing belt vulnerability as the 6G72, plus oil leaks from valve cover gaskets. Rebuilt units include a new timing belt kit, resealed top‑end gaskets, and a new water pump.

entries.title: 4G63 – 2.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: The legendary 4G63 powered the Lancer Evolution I‑IX (1992–2007) and Galant VR‑4. Known for crankshaft walk on early 7‑bolt engines, timing belt failure (interference), and oil starvation in high‑performance applications. Our rebuilt performance engines include upgraded bearings (ARP fasteners), revised oiling system, and a new timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G63 Mitsubishi engine replacement

entries.title: 4G64 – 2.4L Petrol
entries.description: (SOHC/DOHC) Fitted to the Grandis, Delica, L200 (petrol variants), and Space Gear. Known for balance shaft bearing failure and timing belt wear. Rebuilt units include a new timing belt, balance shaft delete option, and uprated bearings.

entries.title: 4G69 – 2.4L Petrol
entries.description: (SOHC MIVEC) Powering the Grandis, Outlander (early), and Lancer. A reliable engine but suffers from timing chain stretch similar to the 4B1 family. Rebuilt units include a new chain kit and upgraded guides. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Mitsubishi Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Mitsubishi's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the legendary 4D56 diesel workhorse and 4G63T turbocharged performance heritage through the 4N-series modern diesel family, the GDI direct-injection petrol era, the 4B11 MIVEC sports engine, and the Outlander PHEV plug-in hybrid system - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Mitsubishi engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: 4D56 Diesel Dominance, 6G72 V6 & Carisma GDI Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The late 1990s Mitsubishi UK range was anchored by the proven 4D56 2.5 turbodiesel across L200 and Shogun, the 6G72 V6 in GTO and Shogun flagship variants, and the Carisma introducing Mitsubishi's pioneering GDI direct-injection petrol technology to European buyers. Key Changes 4D56 2.5 turbodiesel continues as Mitsubishi's primary commercial and off-road diesel - L200, Shogun Sport, Pajero GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) technology introduced via 4G93-GDI in Carisma (1997 launch, UK volume from 1998) - world's first mass-production direct-injection petrol engine 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction 6G72 3.0 V6 SOHC and DOHC continues in Shogun 3.0 V6, GTO / 3000GT Galant and Carisma offer 4G93 1.8 SOHC petrol alongside GDI variants Euro 2 compliance in force across UK range; Euro 3 transition begins Main Engines 4D56 - 2.5 turbodiesel 8v (L200, Shogun Sport, Pajero 2.5 TD) 6G72 - 3.0 V6 SOHC/DOHC petrol (Shogun 3.0, GTO 3.0/3.0 Turbo) 4G93-GDI - 1.8 GDI direct-injection petrol (Carisma GDI) 4G93 - 1.8 SOHC petrol (Carisma, Galant) 6G74 - 3.5 V6 (Shogun 3.5 V6) 4D68 - 2.5 diesel (Galant diesel UK) Popular Models Mitsubishi L200 (second/third generation) Mitsubishi Shogun (third generation) Mitsubishi GTO / 3000GT Mitsubishi Carisma Mitsubishi Galant Known For 4D56 2.5 turbodiesel is one of the most enduring diesel engines in automotive history - claimed manufacturer service life of 200,000 km regularly exceeded in UK working use; however head gasket failure (cracking between valve seats and injectors), crankshaft breakage on high-mileage examples, and timing belt stretch are the three most documented failure modes 6G72 V6 in Shogun and GTO is a smooth, characterful engine - well regarded for durability with correct oil servicing but valve stem seal wear and cam cover gasket oil leaks become routine maintenance items beyond 100,000 miles GDI direct-injection technology in Carisma was genuinely innovative but proved troublesome in UK real-world conditions - carbon build-up on intake valves and injector fouling from low-quality fuel resulted in poor long-term reliability perceptions; UK Carisma specialists consider the GDI system more trouble than it is worth 4D56 timing belt service is critical - original service interval of 90,000 km is widely considered optimistic by UK specialists; 60,000-mile replacement recommended for UK conditions [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 4D56, 4D56T, 6G72, 6G73, 6G74, 4G93, 4G93-GDI, 4D68
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2001 Mitsubishi 4D56 diesel and 6G72 V6 engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Lancer Evolution VII–IX, Outlander & Shogun Fourth Generation 🔴 Performance Landmark
years.description: Short intro: The Lancer Evolution VII, VIII, and IX cemented the 4G63T turbocharged 2.0 as one of the greatest performance engines of the era, while the first-generation Outlander arrived with the new 4G63 SOHC family and the Shogun moved to the fourth generation. Key Changes Lancer Evolution VII (2001), VIII (2003), IX (2005) - 4G63T 2.0 turbo DOHC 280 hp; peak Evo performance era 🔴 Performance Landmark Outlander first generation (2003 UK) - 4G63 2.0 SOHC and 4G69 2.4 MIVEC petrol Shogun fourth generation (2000 launch, UK dominant from 2002) - 4M41 3.2 DiD direct-injection diesel replaces 4D56 in Shogun; 6G74 3.5 V6 petrol continues L200 third generation continues with 4D56 2.5 turbodiesel (8v pre-common rail) Eclipse and Space Star continue with 4G94/4G93 petrol family Euro 3 in force; Euro 4 preparation begins Main Engines 4G63T - 2.0 turbocharged DOHC 280 hp (Lancer Evolution VII, VIII, IX) 4M41 - 3.2 DiD direct-injection diesel (Shogun 3.2 DiD fourth generation) 4G69 - 2.4 MIVEC petrol (Outlander first gen 2.4) 4G63 - 2.0 SOHC petrol (Outlander 2.0) 4D56 - 2.5 turbodiesel (L200 third generation) 6G74 - 3.5 V6 petrol (Shogun 3.5 V6) Popular Models Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution VII / VIII / IX Mitsubishi Shogun (fourth generation) Mitsubishi Outlander (first generation) Mitsubishi L200 (third generation) Mitsubishi Eclipse Known For 4G63T is a legendary turbocharged four-cylinder - robust cast-iron block, exceptional tunability, proven in World Rally Championship; widely considered one of the top five most durable turbocharged performance engines ever produced; oil changes every 3,000–4,000 miles are the key to longevity at high-performance outputs 4M41 3.2 DiD diesel in fourth-generation Shogun is a significant improvement over the 4D56 - direct-injection, stronger architecture; EGR valve clogging and swirl flap failures are the primary UK specialist complaints at 80,000–120,000 miles Evo VIII and IX generate very high UK engine replacement and rebuild enquiry volume - connecting rod bearing wear from modified power levels and oil starvation under lateral G forces are the two most cited failure modes in the performance community 4D56 in L200 at this period is the final pre-common-rail specification - highly robust but head gasket and crankshaft concerns remain; UK workshop feedback considers this the most economical Mitsubishi diesel to maintain [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 4G63T, 4G63, 4M41, 4G69, 4D56, 4D56T, 6G74, 4G94
years.cta: Compare 2002–2005 Mitsubishi Evo, Shogun, and L200 engine prices from UK rebuilders

years.preview: L200 4D56 Common Rail, Lancer Evo X & Eclipse Cross Preview 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: 2006 brought the fourth-generation L200 with the revised 4D56 common-rail diesel - the UK's most important Mitsubishi diesel since its introduction - while the Lancer Evo X previewed the all-new 4B11 turbocharged four-cylinder set to replace the legendary 4G63T. Key Changes L200 fourth generation (2006) launched with 4D56 common-rail 16v 136 hp - significant upgrade over previous 8v unit; dedicated to UK market as primary workhorse 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction Lancer Evo X previewed (2007) with 4B11T 2.0 turbocharged petrol replacing 4G63T 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction ASX/RVR (2007 global, 2010 UK) previewed with 4B10/4B11 MIVEC engine family Outlander second generation (2006) - 4G69 2.4 and 6B31 3.0 V6 petrol options in UK Euro 4 enforced; all diesels require updated fuel system Main Engines 4D56 CR - 2.5 common-rail turbodiesel 16v 136 hp (L200 fourth generation) 4B11T - 2.0 turbocharged petrol (Lancer Evo X - from 2008) 4B11 - 2.0 MIVEC naturally aspirated petrol (Outlander, Lancer) 4G69 - 2.4 MIVEC petrol (Outlander second generation) 6B31 - 3.0 V6 petrol (Outlander 3.0 V6) 4M41 - 3.2 DiD diesel (Shogun fourth generation continued) Popular Models Mitsubishi L200 (fourth generation) Mitsubishi Lancer Evo X Mitsubishi Outlander (second generation) Mitsubishi Shogun (fourth generation) Mitsubishi Colt Known For 4D56 common-rail 16v is a substantial improvement over the 8v unit - more power, better refinement; however the 16v head is more sensitive to overheating than the 8v, and head gasket failures occur from around 100,000 miles if coolant maintenance is neglected 4B11T in Lancer Evo X is well-regarded but generates more modest enthusiasm than its 4G63T predecessor - aluminium block (versus cast iron in 4G63T) makes it lighter but more susceptible to damage under sustained high-boost tuning; stock reliability is excellent 4D56 CR timing belt interval is critical - UK L200 specialists recommend 60,000-mile replacement; neglected belts cause catastrophic engine destruction; this unit generates the highest single volume of UK Mitsubishi engine replacement enquiries Outlander second generation 4G69 is durable but unremarkable - CVT gearbox issues tend to overshadow engine concerns in ownership forums [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 4D56 CR (16v), 4B11T, 4B11, 4G69, 6B31, 4M41, 4G15
years.cta: Get 2006–2008 Mitsubishi L200, Evo X, and Shogun engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: ASX & New Outlander, 4N14 Diesel Introduction & Outlander PHEV Preparation 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The new 4N-series diesel family arrived as Mitsubishi's most advanced diesel architecture to date, the ASX brought a fresh model to UK buyers, and the engineering groundwork for the landmark Outlander PHEV was laid - transforming Mitsubishi's UK positioning. faq.h2: Mitsubishi Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Mitsubishi owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Mitsubishi engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Mitsubishi engine typically costs between £1,500 and £3,500 for a reconditioned unit, depending on the model. A 2.2-litre 4N14 diesel for an Outlander often falls around £2,200–£2,800, while a 3.2-litre 4M41 for a Shogun ranges from £2,500–£3,800. Fitting adds £500–£1,000, with total supply-and-fit costs from £2,000–£4,500. For budget options, a used engine can start from £800–£1,500, but we always recommend asking about warranty and mileage history. Compare Mitsubishi engine replacement prices now.
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi engine prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Mitsubishi engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors influence cost: engine complexity (common 4N14 vs rare 4M40), condition (used vs reconditioned vs rebuilt), vehicle age and accessibility (L200 engine bays are easier than Outlander PHEV), and supply-only vs supply-and-fit. Location matters too – London and South East garages typically charge 20–30% more for fitting. The best way to get a fair price is to compare quotes from different UK specialists on the same engine code. Get quotes from UK Mitsubishi engine specialists.
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi engine prices now

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Mitsubishi?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and 4x4 system are in good shape and the vehicle has genuine sentimental or commercial value. A rebuilt engine with upgraded components (uprated timing chains, revised oil rings, new turbos) often outlasts the original. The alternative – buying a used replacement vehicle of the same age – carries unknown history and often similar risks. For L200s, Shoguns, and Outlander PHEVs that hold strong used values, replacement is almost always the smarter financial decision. Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Mitsubishi engine makes financial sense.

items.question: What's included in a Mitsubishi engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: Full supply and fit includes sourcing a correctly matched engine (on code, not just model), removing the old unit, installing the replacement, and fitting new oils, filters, and gaskets. Most specialists include a 12-month parts and labour warranty, plus disposal of your old engine (core return). A thorough test-drive and final inspection should complete the service. Fitting costs typically add £500–£1,000 to the engine price. Compare Mitsubishi engine supply and fit deals.
items.cta: Get a Mitsubishi engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Mitsubishi engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common models like the L200 (4N15), Outlander (4N14), or ASX (4B11), a straightforward swap takes 3–5 working days. More complex models – Shogun with 4M41, or any PHEV model – can take 5–7 days due to additional systems (transfer box, hybrid cabling). The biggest variable is engine availability: our network stocks commonly replaced codes like 4D56, 4B12, and 4N15. Always ask your chosen specialist for a confirmed lead time before booking. Compare Mitsubishi engine replacement lead times and prices.

items.question: Do reconditioned Mitsubishi engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Rebuilt and remanufactured units often offer extended 24-month coverage, while used engines typically come with 3–6 months. The warranty covers major internal components: crankshaft, pistons, timing chain/belt, oil pump, cylinder head (crack-free). Always confirm exact terms – some warranties exclude fitting work or require a VAT receipt. Find reconditioned Mitsubishi engines with comprehensive warranty cover.

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Mitsubishi engine?
items.answer: Choose a used engine (£800–£1,500) if you need a temporary fix, are selling the vehicle soon, or run a low-value older Mitsubishi with minimal investment risk. Choose reconditioned (£1,800–£3,500 plus fitting) if you plan to keep the vehicle for 2+ years or 60,000 miles – the upfront premium buys new piston rings, bearings, gaskets, and a 12-month warranty. For known failure-prone engines (4N14 HP pump, 4B11 timing chain, 4M40 thrust washers), a reconditioned unit with upgraded parts is strongly recommended. items.question: What are the most common Mitsubishi engine failures?
items.answer: The five most common failures driving UK replacement demand are: 4N14 HP pump disintegration (metal contamination of entire fuel system); 4B11/4B12 timing chain stretch (rattling on start-up, eventual jump); 4M40/4M41 crankshaft thrust washer failure (axial crank movement, block scrap); 4A92/3B20 oil control ring seizure (1 litre per 600 miles consumption); and 4N15 EGR/turbo clogging (loss of power, DPF block). Early detection rarely prevents these from becoming catastrophic – replacement is the most common outcome. Compare replacement prices for these common failure engines.

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Mitsubishi engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes – prices for the same engine code can vary by £1,000+ across UK suppliers. Consider used (£800–£1,500) for tight budgets, but reconditioned with upgraded parts (£1,800–£3,500) offers better long-term value. Ask suppliers to break down supply-only vs supply-and-fit, and confirm whether delivery, fitting, and VAT are included in the quoted price. For L200s and Shoguns, weekday fitting slots can cost less than weekend bookings. Use our comparison tool to check live prices across our network.
